retrieved_docs.title: Hysterosalpingogram -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: This activity for healthcare professionals is designed to enhance knowledge regarding a hysterosalpingogram procedure, including its purpose, indications, and techniques, from patient positioning to contrast media considerations, to ensure clinicians grasp the procedure's intricacies. Ultimately, this activity will provide the competence and skills necessary to effectively utilize this imaging modality in the diagnosis and management of female infertility, thereby improving patient outcomes and quality of care.

retrieved_docs.content: Fill the uterine cavity with radioopaque media, typically 1 to 3 mL initially, and perform multiple pelvic x-rays to visualize the spread of the media from the uterine cavity into the fallopian tubes, eventually reaching fimbriated ends next to the ovaries.

retrieved_docs.title: Hysterosalpingogram -- Personnel
retrieved_docs.content: HSG is most commonly performed in a radiology suite by a radiology technician. However, an OB/GYN clinician can also perform the procedure in the office or clinic setting. In most cases, only a single operator is required to perform this procedure.

retrieved_docs.title: Hysteroscopy -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: Objectives: Identify the indications for hysteroscopy. Apply recommended technique to perform hysteroscopy. Implement appropriate management of complications associated with hysteroscopy. Apply interprofessional team strategies to improve care coordination and outcomes in patients undergoing hysteroscopy. Access free multiple choice questions on this topic.

retrieved_docs.title: Hysterosalpingogram -- Preparation
retrieved_docs.content: HSG should be ideally scheduled during the first half of the menstrual cycle, days 1 to 14, to reduce the chance of concurrent pregnancy. Pain mitigation strategies include prophylactic oral nonsteroidal anti-inflammatory medications and endocervical local anesthesia. Often, clinicians administer an over-the-counter pain reliever in the nonsteroidal anti-inflammatory drug class (eg, naproxen sodium, ibuprofen, or indomethacin) an hour before the procedure. However, studies have not demonstrated significant pain reduction using prophylaxis. [14] [15]

retrieved_docs.title: Hysterosalpingogram -- Technique or Treatment -- Hysterosalpingogram Imaging
retrieved_docs.content: The exact number of the images obtained is institution-dependent. Filling of the endometrial cavity is continued until the media is observed to flow from each fallopian tube (see Images. Patent Fallopian Tubes and Bilateral Patent Fallopian Tubes). The hysterosalpingogram mainly visualizes the areas with continued patency to the uterine cavity where the contrast is deposited (see Image. Hysterosalpingogram Image Following Contrast Media Installation). In cases where at least 1 fallopian tube does not demonstrate contrast flow, intravenous anticholinergic medication can be given to rule out the possibility of fallopian tube smooth muscle spasms. The contrast media appears hyperdense in imaging, thus allowing for the visualization of the media’s pathway. Failure to see the expected flow of contrast media into the peritoneal cavity from either fallopian tube is evidence of an occlusion. See Images. Right Fallopian Tube Occlusion, Left Fallopian Tube Occlusion, Bilateral Fallopian Tube Occlusion, Endometrial Cavity Image on Hysterosalpingogram, and Peritoneal Dye Spillage on Hysterosalpingogram for examples of contrast flow imaging and interpretation.

retrieved_docs.title: Hysteroscopy -- Technique or Treatment -- Insertion of the Hysteroscope and Uterine Cavity Assessment
retrieved_docs.content: Once inside the uterus, the entire uterine cavity may be inspected. Any pathology can be quickly identified, and preoperative planning can be performed. The bilateral tubal ostia should be identified at the beginning of the case.

retrieved_docs.title: Sonography Gynecology Infertility Assessment, Protocols, and Interpretation -- Technique or Treatment -- Saline Infusion Sonogram
retrieved_docs.content: If tubal patency assessment is planned or required following the intrauterine assessment, a sonohysterosalpingogram (hysterosalpingo contrast sonography -HyCoSy) is performed. During HyCoSy, an agitated mixture of air and normal saline is injected into the uterine cavity through the SIS catheter while performing a real-time ultrasound. Attention is paid to the cornual ends bilaterally to visualize the passage of fluid with air bubbles through the ostia of the fallopian tubes and noting the spilling of the mixed fluid into the adnexa region or elsewhere in the abdominal cavity (Figure 9).

The concept of “Organ” connects these procedures to their anatomical targets, linking the excised tissues (e.g., vaginal biopsy, fine‑needle ovarian biopsy) to the organs themselves and to endoscopic access methods (pelvic endoscopy, uterine hysteroscopy). This integrated hierarchy provides a systematic framework for the histopathological evaluation of gynecologic and ovarian tissues, guiding clinicians in selecting appropriate diagnostic approaches and ensuring accurate tissue sampling within obstetric‑gynecologic care.

These three procedures fall within the scope of the Obstetrics & Gynecology specialty, illustrating the range of diagnostic and surgical techniques that obstetric‑gynecologic physicians use to evaluate and treat uterine, cervical, and adnexal conditions.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 22 -- Level 3 -- Community 1939
retrieved_docs.content: Imaging procedures related to the genital and breast organs encompass a range of techniques, with vaginoscopy (منظار المهبل) identified as a specific sub‑procedure within this category. In the clinical setting of a twin gestation (gestation twin), there is a notable association with the need for other excision of the cervix and uterus, reflecting the potential for cervical or uterine pathology that may arise or be uncovered during a multiple‑pregnancy course. Moreover, the excision of the cervix and uterus frequently co‑occurs with vaginoscopy, indicating that visualization of the vaginal canal and cervical region is often performed in conjunction with surgical removal procedures. This interrelationship underscores the importance of integrating diagnostic imaging (including vaginoscopy) with therapeutic excision techniques when managing complex obstetric cases such as twin pregnancies, thereby facilitating comprehensive assessment and optimal patient care.

retrieved_docs.content: Physicians specializing in Obstetrics & Gynecology are trained to perform salpingo‑oophorectomy procedures, which may involve complete or partial removal of the fallopian tubes and ovaries and can be conducted unilaterally or bilaterally as a separate surgical operation.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 5 -- Level 3 -- Community 2361
retrieved_docs.content: Salpingo‑oophorectomy, complete or partial, unilateral or bilateral (separate procedure) is closely related to oophorectomy; unilateral and bilateral, as both surgical interventions involve removal of ovarian tissue and may be performed on one side (unilateral) or both sides (bilateral).

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 18 -- Level 4 -- Community 2760
retrieved_docs.content: إزالة المبيض جزئيًا (partial oophorectomy) and استئصال الرحم جزئيًا (partial hysterectomy) are gynecologic surgical procedures that frequently co‑occur, often being performed together in the management of conditions requiring simultaneous partial removal of ovarian and uterine tissue. This co‑occurrence reflects a clinical approach where both organs are addressed in a single operative session to treat disease, reduce patient morbidity, and streamline postoperative recovery.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 34 -- Level 3 -- Community 2409
retrieved_docs.content: Aspiration curettage of uterus for termination of pregnancy, hysterotomy and termination of pregnancy, surgical treatment of miscarriage of any trimester, surgical treatment of missed abortion of the second trimester, and surgical treatment of missed miscarriage of the third trimester are all medical procedures that are directly related to إجهاض (abortion). Within this broader category, hysterotomy and termination of pregnancy as well as surgical treatment of miscarriage of any trimester represent narrower, more specific forms of abortion care. The procedures for missed abortion in the second trimester and missed miscarriage in the third trimester are also explicitly linked to إجهاض, highlighting their role in managing pregnancy loss at different stages.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 33 -- Level 3 -- Community 1853

This overarching group of female‑organ diagnostic interventions includes procedures such as تنظيم جوف الحوض (pelvic endoscopy), خزعة المهبل (vaginal biopsy), and منظار الرحم (uterine hysteroscopy). Together, these procedures represent specialized diagnostic techniques used to evaluate the cervix, vagina, uterus, and surrounding pelvic structures in female patients, facilitating accurate assessment and management of gynecologic conditions.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 13 -- Level 4 -- Community 2845
retrieved_docs.content: The female reproductive system encompasses surgical interventions such as abdominal hysterectomy, a procedure in which the uterus is removed through an abdominal incision. Abdominal hysterectomy frequently co‑occurs with salpingo‑oophorectomy, the removal of the fallopian tube and ovary, indicating that these gynecologic surgeries are often performed together. This combined approach is commonly employed in the management of conditions affecting the uterus and adnexal structures, such as malignant or benign tumors, severe endometriosis, or other complex pelvic diseases, and informs surgical planning and patient counseling in women’s health care.

Diagnostic interventions on the female reproductive system fall under “other diagnostic procedures; female organs,” a subcategory of “procedure for female reproductive organs,” and comprise pelvic endoscopy (تنظير جوف الحوض), vaginal biopsy (خزبة المهبل), uterine hysteroscopy (منظار الرحم), closed uterine biopsy, endocervical biopsy, open uterine biopsy, and other cervical diagnostic procedures. Coding guidelines prohibit concurrent coding of irrigation of implanted venous access devices with endometrial sampling (with or without endocervical sampling), excision of cervical stump by abdominal approach, any other therapeutic procedures for female organs, phototherapy (المعالجة بالضوء فوق البنفسجي), or tympanoplasty. Aspiration curettage performed after delivery or abortion is classified under dilatation and curettage (D&C) as “Aspiration curettage following delivery or abortion.” Irrigation of an implanted

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 3 -- Level 3 -- Community 2488
retrieved_docs.content: Endometrial sampling (biopsy) with or without endocervical sampling (biopsy), without cervical dilation, any method (separate procedure) and Salpingo‑oophorectomy, complete or partial, unilateral or bilateral (separate procedure) are both procedures that fall within the scope of Physicians – Obstetrics & Gynecology. Physicians specialized in Obstetrics & Gynecology are trained to perform endometrial sampling biopsies as part of uterine evaluation and to conduct salpingo‑oophorectomy, whether complete or partial and unilateral or bilateral. Recognizing that these two distinct procedures are performed by obstetric‑gynecologic physicians guides appropriate referral, scheduling, and patient counseling.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 18 -- Level 3 -- Community 1603
retrieved_docs.content: Obstetrics excision is a surgical category that includes a range of procedures used to manage pregnancy loss and delivery. Within this category, “Hysterotomy and termination of pregnancy” and “Induced termination of pregnancy following intra‑amniotic injection with hysteresis” are specific techniques for ending a pregnancy surgically. Obstetrics excision also encompasses the “Surgical treatment of miscarriage of any trimester,” providing a universal approach to miscarriage care, as well as the “Surgical treatment of missed abortion of second trimester,” which addresses missed abortions specifically in the second trimester. In addition, the category includes “إنهاء الحمل خارج الرحم” (termination of an ectopic pregnancy) and “ولادة قيصرية في الجزء السفلي” (lower‑segment cesarean delivery), extending its scope to the surgical management of ectopic pregnancies and the performance of cesarean sections. Together, these procedures illustrate the comprehensive role of obstetric excision techniques in safely terminating pregnancies, treating various forms of miscarriage, and delivering infants when vaginal birth is not feasible.
